deck: The script flips: Jonathan Jackson interviews Amie Vaccaro on her path into social-impact marketing and the lessons from building marketing as a horizontal, foundational team at Dimagi, with lessons for anyone leading a shared service.
seo_description: Jonathan Jackson interviews Amie Vaccaro on her career and building marketing as a horizontal shared service at Dimagi, with lessons for any digital team.
og_description: Building marketing as a horizontal shared service at Dimagi, and what it teaches anyone leading digital across an organization.
about: In this episode Dimagi flips the script: CEO and co-founder Jonathan Jackson interviews co-host Amie Vaccaro, Senior Director of Marketing, about her career journey and the lessons she has learned growing the marketing team at Dimagi. Amie traces her path from studying psychology, through management consulting and social-impact work, to product marketing roles at companies including Salesforce, Change.org, and Autodesk, before joining Dimagi.

The conversation draws a parallel that runs throughout the episode: digital only unlocks its potential for iNGOs and governments when it becomes a foundational, horizontal layer that supports every part of an organization, rather than a siloed vertical project. Amie has been building marketing the same way at Dimagi, as a shared service that supports all six divisions.

Amie and Jonathan unpack the hard parts of standing up a horizontal capability: earning trust and buy-in, balancing big strategic bets against day-to-day requests, empowering a team to push back, and shifting the conversation from whether a request is useful to whether it is the most valuable use of time.

pull_quote.text: Good shared services should have an infinite backlog. That's why they make sense to create a shared service in the first place. And shifting into a value-based discussion is the key unlock.
pull_quote.cite: Jonathan Jackson, on running a horizontal team

bullets: Flipping the script: Jonathan interviews Amie on the career path from psychology and consulting to social-impact product marketing
Digital as a horizontal foundation: why projects fail when they stay siloed in one vertical and cannot add value across an organization
Earning the mandate: building buy-in and trust with no clear mandate, and why Jonathan made Amie earn it
Big rocks vs. blocking and tackling: balancing step-change strategic work against the constant flow of division requests
From reactive to proactive: shifting marketing from executing requests to partnering on strategy and understanding the real problem
Useful vs. valuable: why almost every ask is useful, and how a value-based discussion is the key to prioritizing a shared service
One Dimagi: lifting all boats, over-communicating, and tying every effort to impact, team, and profit

stats.label: people on the marketing team today, up from 3 when Amie joined
business divisions at Dimagi that marketing supports horizontally
standalone digital health applications once in use across Malawi, cited as the siloed-vendor problem
